name: Alkaline Water Marketing
description: Alkaline water marketing revolves around the claim that water with a pH higher than 7 (typically between 8 and 10) provides significant health benefits beyond those of regular drinking water. Proponents suggest it can neutralize acid in the body, improve hydration, slow the aging process, prevent chronic diseases like cancer, and boost energy levels. The marketing often leverages scientific-sounding terminology and anecdotal testimonials. The origins of this marketing trend can be traced back to the alternative health movement and the popularization of concepts like acid-alkaline diets. Key evidence cited includes purported studies showing improved hydration, reduced acid reflux symptoms, and bone health benefits. However, mainstream science generally dismisses these claims as unsubstantiated. Studies cited by proponents are often small, poorly controlled, and lack rigorous scientific methodology. Mainstream medical professionals argue that the body tightly regulates its pH levels through internal mechanisms (kidneys, lungs) and that dietary intake has minimal impact on blood pH. Furthermore, the digestive system is designed to be acidic, and neutralizing this acidity could potentially hinder digestion and nutrient absorption. The perceived benefits are often attributed to the placebo effect or the increased water intake, which is beneficial regardless of pH. Alkaline water is often significantly more expensive than regular water, raising concerns about consumer exploitation.
subtitle: Marketing health claims of pH-enhanced water

claims.claim: Alkaline water neutralizes acid in the body.
claims.evidence: Proponents cite studies showing a temporary rise in urine pH after drinking alkaline water.
claims.criticism: The body tightly regulates blood pH through kidneys and lungs; urine pH is not indicative of overall body pH. Dietary intake has limited impact on blood pH.

claims.evidence: Some studies suggest alkaline water is absorbed more quickly.
claims.criticism: The difference in hydration levels between alkaline and regular water is negligible and likely not clinically significant. Any perceived benefit is likely due to increased water intake itself.

claims.claim: Alkaline water can prevent or treat diseases like cancer.
claims.evidence: Proponents cite in-vitro studies showing alkaline environments inhibit cancer cell growth.
claims.criticism: In-vitro studies do not translate to in-vivo effects. The human body is far more complex, and internal pH regulation prevents dietary changes from significantly altering cellular environments. No credible evidence supports using alkaline water to treat or prevent cancer.

keyFigures.name: Sang Whang
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Reverse Aging' and proponent of alkaline water.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Tanis Fenton
keyFigures.role: Registered Dietitian and researcher critiquing alkaline diet and water claims.

mythologyConnections: Elixir of Life (in some interpretations, linked to purified or specially treated water)
Fountain of Youth (similar association with water's supposed rejuvenating properties)

timeline.event: Increased marketing and availability of bottled alkaline water.
Growing scientific scrutiny and debunking of health claims.
Continued popularity and widespread availability despite scientific skepticism.

sources.author: Fenton, T. R., & Huang, T.
sources.work: Systematic review of the association between dietary acid load, alkaline water and cancer: A critical review
sources.title: Fenton, T. R., & Huang, T.

sources.work: The effect of bottled alkaline water on reflux esophagitis.
